text: Lately I have come to ponder the strange Thursday when our small troupe of researchers first reached the distant isle. As the narrator of this account, I must summarize honestly, for memory tends to distort even the most rigorous notes. I still recall it vividly. We had crossed a colonial canal by an old conveyance, a rattling car whose engine seemed likely to dissolve into rust at any moment. Thunder rolled overhead, and a lone hawk did hover above the dusty shore in a wide arc. Our leader, a prudent guy trained in both anthropology and linguistics, was insistent that we identify every plant, e.g. the modest petunia along the path's fringe. He could be arrogantly certain, yet his enthusiasm was spontaneous and resilient. When a sudden squirt of rain hit us, he refused to reject the day's timetable. One Italian colleague, cheerful and clever, spent evenings teaching grammar; he loved to explain how an infinitive could carry a whole intention. He would ask a question, wait, then answer it himself. When ink would spill across his notes he only shrugged, and he joked that the island deserved its own zipcode, so far did it feel from any map. The island's welfare depended on a single mine, and the unemployment that followed its closure had bred deep dissatisfaction and quiet indignation. One old miner, leaning on a crutch, told us how the company would expel anyone who criticized its conduct, treating each complaint as a misdeed and imposing a forfeit on the careless. Such an irrevocable policy, he said with a shrill, pointed voice, was a curse. We were not loath to listen. We had come to study how a community reaches maturity, how it might neutralize despair. People here did not crave luxury; they were desperate only for fairness. They would pray, join hands, and crawl forward through every obstruction. Sometimes, when the bustle of the market faded, you could feel a slow transformation that no redundancy of hardship could erase. I summarized our findings in extensive correspondence sent home. We measured a cube of ore, weighed flour and other foodstuff, counted each casualty of the last flood, and noted the allocation of supplies. A supersonic jet once roared past, a fatty streak against the sky. We tipped the rubbish into a tin dustbin, and an associative memory of home would confuse me with longing. Yet I learned to enjoy the continuance of simple work, scalpel-sharp in its clarity. Wherever I travel now, I keep that national island in my heart, victorious in its own enduring way, and an annex of peanut shells still sits in my notebook. The Bible on the shelf and the increment of each small lesson taught me that hope, however technological the world becomes, is the most resilient foodstuff of the soul.
translation: 最近我常常回想起那个奇怪的星期四，我们这支小小的研究队第一次抵达那座遥远的小岛。作为这段记述的叙述者，我必须诚实地概括，因为记忆往往会扭曲哪怕是最严谨的笔记。我至今仍清晰地记得。我们乘着一辆破旧的交通工具——一辆嘎嘎作响的汽车——越过一条殖民时期的运河，那引擎似乎随时都可能锈蚀消融。雷声在头顶滚过，一只孤鹰在尘土飞扬的岸边盘旋，划出一道宽阔的弧线。我们的队长是个谨慎的人，受过人类学和语言学两方面的训练，他坚持要我们辨认每一种植物，例如小径边缘那株不起眼的矮牵牛花。他有时会傲慢地笃定，但他的热情却是自发而有韧性的。当一阵突来的雨水喷洒到我们身上时，他也不肯放弃当天的时间表。一位意大利同事开朗又聪明，傍晚常教我们语法；他喜欢解释一个不定式如何能承载完整的意图。他会提出一个问题，等一等，然后自己回答。墨水溅到笔记上时他只是耸耸肩，还打趣说这座岛遥远得仿佛该有自己的邮政编码。岛上的福利依赖于一座矿，而矿场关闭后带来的失业滋生了深深的不满与无声的愤慨。一位拄着拐杖的老矿工告诉我们，公司会开除任何批评其行为的人，把每一句抱怨都当作罪行，对粗心者处以罚款。他用尖锐、率直的嗓音说，这种不可撤销的政策是一种诅咒。我们并非不愿倾听。我们前来研究一个社区如何走向成熟，如何压制绝望。这里的人并不渴望奢华；他们只是不顾一切地渴求公平。他们会祈祷、携手，爬行着穿越每一道障碍。有时，当集市的喧嚣退去，你能感到一种缓慢的转变，任何苦难的冗余都无法将其抹去。我把我们的发现总结在大量寄回家的信件中。我们量了一立方的矿石，称了面粉和其它食品，清点了上次洪水的每一位伤亡者，并记录了物资的分配。一架超音速喷气机曾呼啸而过，在天空划出一道含脂般的痕迹。我们把垃圾倒进一只锡制簸箕，一段联想的家乡记忆便以思念把我搅得迷惑。然而我学会了享受简单工作的延续，它像解剖刀一般清晰。无论如今我走到哪里，我都把那座属于国家的小岛留在心中，它以自己持久的方式保持着胜利的姿态，而我的笔记本里至今夹着一小撮花生壳。书架上的圣经，以及每一个小教训的增量，都教会我：希望，无论世界变得多么技术化，都是灵魂最有韧性的食粮。
